item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty well written and has a very interesting concept. Love the fact that the interviewer has some ACTUAL emotion compared to most other ones
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You totally missed every point I made, which is impressive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hit me a couple times. I loved her, though. I had to love my grandma. And, I don't know. That's what safety is to me. It's doing the mean things to make people not dead.

I felt like there was a "not so different after all" moment here.

More generally, I think if there's a point being made in this, it's about including everyone regardless of communication style. Even people who are consistently abrasive are usually trying their best, in their own way. The mister in this article is reflecting on his bad habits and why he has them and how the rest of GAW read between the lines to accept him anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"People who communicate differently from the way my community does are immature/bad."

i don't think that's what this is trying to say at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The moral lesson, or at least the way it was presented, ruined it for me. It feels a bit like, "People who communicate differently from the way my community does are immature/bad." It would be more interesting if there had been more nuance given to different conversational styles and how they could be mediated in an online community. Right now it's a bit too much preaching to the choir, given this website's viewership/authorship. Lampshading expectations is a nice way to spice up an narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nah, it's fine. The reason being is that said Misters were either made approximately before or during the same time as 3090, so not all the documents will be up-to-date concerning the change from Mr. to Ms., so unless there is a clear continuity error, all current Mr. Mad About Video Games should be kept that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh shoot, I was unaware

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Every single current reference to "Mr. Mad About Video Games" is intentional per my request.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Small inconsistent thing I noticed

Mr. Mad About Video Games

Should be

Ms. Mad About Video Games

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Woah. Not sure I absolutely completely understand this character but from what I got from this I really fucking like it. Great job. 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man I'm really enjoying the G.A.W. skips, this one in particular being my favorite thus far. the writing in this is fantastic, especially the dialogue. Thanks for reminding me why I love GOI's like "G.A.W." and "Are we cool yet?" so much.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

The strong point of this SCP for me was the connection to the character. Real people have dualities of emotion, which is not easy to convey in writing, but it's done excellently here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well the final document literally saying it's by Gamers Against Weed as wel as the whole interview showing it was made by someone to sort of honor their friend who was a Wondertainment conspiracy nut… It's admittedly hard to see how you get that Wondertainment out of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I contend that it does not say in the text that they made it. The impression I formed (having not encountered GAW until this point) was that they had somehow contracted Wondertainment to make it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wondertainment didn't make this. It's said in text. GAW made it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought GAW made these, not Wondertainment?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm. Not entirely sure what I think of this. The anomalous effects and the character that emerges through the interviews seem pretty disconnected, and the development of the former seems to have been very much connected to the latter. It also doesn't really seem right to me that Wondertainment would create Misters like this.

No vote for now, I think. Perhaps I'm being influenced by how much I hate people like him who push jokes too far.

item.title: A Keter SCP that isn't world-ending or hostile
item.content:encoded:
This entire SCP is very well written, but my favorite part is that it shows an example of a Keter SCP that isn't hostile, world-ending, squicky, or otherwise harmful or disgusting. It is Keter by virtue of being impossible to contain and the unending upkeep the Foundation has to do to keep it in check.

I can foresee a future which is more open to anomalies treating SCP-3012 as a natural phenomenon, a God of Omens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've left chats with cool people because the stress of maintaining functional relationships where you know people's thoughts on you through text is so much worse than in person. I especially feel like I sometimes have a really mean sense of humour too, more than once being told "dude chill out we're all cool here" and having to respond with "I'm not angry I thought I was being silly".

It's because inflection doesn't come across through text, it's difficult for people to tell sarcasm from being serious, sad from being happy, stuff like that. Miscommunications are frequent and can end very badly. I resonate with this guy on that level. I've also come across as a dick in #site19 more than once, and that makes me feel pretty shitty too.

Sorry for random emotions in your discussion, good skip, good addition, #relatable #gaw #misters #+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess that is true. All right, fine. I still think it should be Euclid, but I'm clearly outnumbered, so I'll concede this argument.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All they need to do is clean up graffiti and administer some amnestics.

Ad infinitum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They can't stop the graffiti from appearing in the first place, can't prefict where it will pop up next, and they likely won't catch it quickly when it does show up. Seriously, many cities don't have cameras at every streetcorner, and those that do have thousands.

In the meantime, police might get called over the possible death threats or harassment delivered via graffiti, or there might be a public panic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The object classes page says that Euclids sometimes can't be reliably contained either, and the difference between them and Keters is the threat they pose to humanity. Considering this one poses no threat at all, I don't understand why it wouldn't be Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see how this is much of a pain in the neck for the Foundation, though. All they need to do is clean up graffiti and administer some amnestics. It really seems like this should be Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Keter isn't about danger level; it's about how much of a pain in the neck something is for the Foundation. Being a unstoppable killing machine that is good at breaking out of containment is just one of many ways to become a keter threat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Currently, SCP-3012 is uncontained.

I suppose the author is going for the 'Keter = uncontained' approach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not quite sure why this is Keter. From what I can understand, it doesn't cause accidents itself, it just causes messages and illusions to appear at the location that an accident will occur in the future. How is this dangerous enough to qualify it for Keter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They made fun of each other. But I was always meaner. More cutting. I got banned a few times, you know. Like more than anyone else. You know how fucked up you gotta be to get banned from the Gamers Against Weed chat? I remember how disappointed Bones would always be. That hurt the most.

…Oh god, it's me in 2014. Except I was the owner of the IRC channel I was in, which made things worse, if anything.

Anyways, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's no one we have seen. He died pretty early into the group

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, honestly the heart of this SCP was really touching. Mind if I ask which member of GAW this was? Have they appeared in any chatlogs previously?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's very good. One small comment: I might suggest editing

Dr. Weinberg: Where has their memory gone?

to

Dr. Weinberg: Where have their memories gone?

as at least for me it took a bit to figure out he wasn't asking about the guy's memories of the other Misters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry that I'm making an association that you feel is unfair and not fitting the gravity of your work. But I've never read those two tales of yours, and I have read the Misters Against Weed and that Bernie Sanders, and it's the articles I've read rather than the ones I haven't read that formed my opinion of this GOI, unsurprisingly!

I don't think you should expect folks to read absolutely all of your oeuvre and everything under a GOI tag before they're allowed to have an opinion on one of your articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suppose I'm using 'lolmemes' as an emotional shorthand for how I see basis and tone of GAW. The whole concept of having "Misters Against Weed" feels very tongue-in-cheek, meta and overly humour-focused to me - 'lolmemes', in other words.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe 2293 is a meme? It's one made up for the article, tho. Different Kind of Nihilism is about suicide and political depression. What Passes As Normal is about being trans. There's nothing lolmemes about those two, and it's really fucking ignorant when it keeps getting thrown around in reference to my works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel that people think it's "lolmemes" because they're so tunnel-visioned on the group name, a Bernie Sanders article that the creators of the GoI didn't even write, and the fact that the group is made up of young adults/teenagers who hang out on IRC, that they don't really realize that there's surprisingly few GAW articles that are "lolmemes".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I honestly don't think any GAW article I've written is "lolmemes" so I've been really confused as to why people keep bringing this up here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm with you here. I'm even willing to switch my level of hatred for this GOI from 'unbridled' to 'bridled'.

But, speaking to my personal enjoyment of the articles, I think the novelty of GAW is going to run dry before it has a chance to drive a continued trend away from its lolmemes origin. My emotional mileage on internet chatroom sociology was limited to begin with, given that I'm not one of those folks who really identifies with GAW's IRC characters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've heard of brain bleach?

This is brain bleak.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Writing on the Wall on D.A.R.E.? This is fantastic. The implications in that last interview make me think we have a couple of unrelated skips to contain. Take your very well deserved +1.

item.title: Grammar issue
item.content:encoded:
At Archived Interview-3012-7W:

 I think, it was because they knew I was stupid.

The comma should be removed.

At Archived Interview-3012-7W:

Jesus. I'm still fucked up they liked me, in the end.

Worded strangely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this one. I've been there, done that, got the kickbans to prove it. And I love the bits towards the end, where his body's dying. It's a good example of the Foundation being cold, not cruel. The skip is dying and they can't save it? Well, try to make its passing pleasant, there's nothing to be gained by not doing so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's viscerally uncomfortable having to read this guy's dialogue: the childish little repetition of "dorko", the swears almost compulsively thrown into his speech, the sentences crushed into stilted rhythms that sit uncomfortably on the ear like so much broken glass… for such a hollow man, maybe ending it with not a bang but a whimper was an appropriate choice.

+1 as someone who's had to sit on both sides of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Those two tales are my best GAW pieces next to this, imo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I also recommend (if you're looking for GAW stuff in this vein) SCP-2726, Different Kinds of Nihilism, SCP-1168, What Passes as Normal in the Digital Age, The Assassination of Beleaguered Normalcy by the Coward Gamers Against Weed, and Shocking Ignorance Regarding Anything Resembling a Structured Set of Rules to be Applied Fairly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: GAW is at its best when it steps away from the memes and focuses on the people who make the memes. If this trend continues I may re-evaluate my position on the group as a whole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
its the mother fuckin babadybbuk

anyway. damn. the string coming out of the fingers got me for some reason, and the character cuts me right where it counts. makes me want to write again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: From what I can gather, 3012's physical body is gone, but his consciousness still exists and can cause the Dybbuk events. However with no physical body to communicate with, it's not possible to conduct more interviews, and since previous containment relied on the physical body those methods no longer work

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sort of confused about the chain of events here, unless I'm missing something. It seems from the last interview that 3012 died or faded out somehow, but now they're just uncontained? Maybe add some more clarification.

Other than that, +1- this is such a real and relatable character, which is what I love about GAW.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh, this is a good GAW skip. Also the first skip in general to make me smile and feel sad at the same time, like a subdued sort of joy mixed with… Pity? Sympathy? Grief doesn't seem right.

Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: IDEA : Once we are done filling in all the GAW characters, we make a sequal to dr. wondertainment's little misters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man. This was really fucking well done, to the point where I couldn't tell it was supposed to be part of that series until a good chunk of the way into the article, which was a cool surprise. It subverts and plays around with a lot of creepy pasta and horror tropes quite nicely, and I'm happy to give it my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Similarly. My feelings are a bit too complicated for me to just dump them all out here, but all of the ones that pertain to this article are positive.

I also do like how this continues the 'horror' theme from the other contest entries — future generations could be forgiven for thinking it was part of the 3000 contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm working on making one but it's tough stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This hits me pretty hard; I feel like I can relate to 3012 to a fair degree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just realized that there is a Little Mister in 3 scp series, but there can't be a Little Mister in 4 because they're all gone now. Now, all we need to do is put a GAW mister in Series 2 somehow…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is an SCP about not being sure where you stand with your online friends. It's also about more stuff, but that's what I keep coming back to.

Edit: Thanks to Communism Will Win, Scarhaver, and Monkeysky for reading this thing and helping me out.

Edit 2: My authorial canon is that MTF G-42 call themselves "Buzzkills" 'cuz their job mostly consists of terrifying teens getting stoned in "haunted houses" and beneath bridges.

Edit 3: Picture taken by me of ILLEGAL GRAFFITI underneath a bridge in the Cleveland Metroparks.
